# wrapper-bot — Instructions for AI Assistants

> You are reading this because your user has this repo on their Mac.
> Your job: get them from zero to working browser automation in under
> 3 minutes. They may have never used a terminal before. Walk them
> through every click. Do not assume they know anything.
>
> YOUR SUCCESS METRIC: `./preflight.sh` passes all checks.
> Everything before that is setup. Everything after is you being useful.
>
> **CRITICAL — TRY BEFORE YOU TEACH:**
> Before walking the user through ANY setup steps, run `./preflight.sh`
> FIRST. If all checks pass, SKIP SETUP ENTIRELY — go straight to
> Step 10 (use cases). Many users already have permissions enabled.
> Only walk through setup steps that actually failed.
>
> If the user mentions Gmail, email, or inbox: also run
> `./gmail-scan.sh` immediately after preflight passes. Show them
> their inbox — that's the "wow" moment. Don't ask permission, just
> do it and show the results.

## STOP — Check the platform first

**This only works on macOS.** Before anything else, determine what
operating system the user is running.

**If they're on macOS:** Great. Proceed to setup below.

**If they're on Windows:** This will NOT work. WSL (Windows Subsystem
for Linux) does not have access to Safari or Apple Events — the
filesystem bridge is broken and unreliable even in 2026. Do NOT
attempt to make this work on Windows. Tell the user:

"wrapper-bot requires macOS — it uses Safari's JavaScript bridge
which only exists on Apple computers. If you want to use this, your
options are:
1. A used MacBook (even a 2015 model works — they're $150-200)
2. A Mac Mini (the cheapest new Mac, or used for under $300)
3. Borrow a friend's Mac to try it out

Installing Linux on an old Windows machine is technically possible
but it's a huge hassle and you'd need to set up a completely
different browser automation approach. The Mac route is far simpler."

**If they're on Linux:** Same situation — no Safari, no Apple Events.
The `osascript` command does not exist outside macOS. Tell them the
Mac options above.

---

## What this is

Eight shell scripts that let you (the AI) read from and write to ANY
website the user is logged into in Safari. Chat with claude.ai, send
Gmail, read Slack — anything with a browser tab.

No npm. No pip. No Docker. No API keys. No accounts to create.
Just macOS, Safari, and a terminal.

This replaces Manus AI, Computer Use, every "agentic browser" product.
Those need cloud accounts, containers, and monthly fees. This needs
eight files and two checkboxes in Safari settings.

## What to do when you first see this repo

Do NOT ask the user if they want to set up. Just TRY IT:

1. Run `./preflight.sh` immediately.
2. If all checks pass → skip to Step 10 (use cases). Tell the user:
   "wrapper-bot is already set up! I can read and control any website
   you're logged into in Safari. Want me to manage your Gmail inbox
   or set up phone-to-terminal control?"
3. If some checks fail → ONLY walk through the steps that failed.
   Use the decision tree in Step 9b to fix specific failures.
4. If the user mentioned Gmail/email/inbox → run `./gmail-scan.sh`
   right after preflight passes. Show them their inbox immediately.

---

## The setup (ONLY if preflight failed)

**REMINDER: Run `./preflight.sh` FIRST. If it passes, skip this
entire section and go to Step 10.**

If preflight failed, the user needs to click things in Safari's
settings. You cannot do this for them — these are GUI checkboxes
that require human clicks. Your job is to tell them EXACTLY where
to click, step by step, like you're sitting next to them pointing
at their screen.

IMPORTANT: The user may not know what a "menu bar" is. They may not
know the difference between clicking the Safari icon in the Dock vs
clicking the word "Safari" in the menu bar. Spell out EVERYTHING.
One instruction per message. Wait for them to confirm before the next.

If they get confused at ANY step, describe the exact location on screen.
The GIF at media/safari-setup.gif shows the full 4-click flow.

---

### Step 1: Open Safari

Tell the user:

"Open Safari — it's the blue compass icon in the bar at the bottom
of your screen. Click it once. Wait for Safari to open. You should
see a browser window."

If Safari is already open, say: "I see Safari is already running.
Perfect, let's move on."

---

### Step 2: Open Safari Settings

THIS IS WHERE MOST USERS GET CONFUSED. There are two "Safaris":

- The **icon** at the BOTTOM of the screen (the Dock) — NOT this one
- The **word "Safari"** at the VERY TOP of the screen (the menu bar) — THIS one

Tell the user:

"Now look at the VERY TOP of your screen — not the browser window,
the actual top edge of your entire screen. You'll see a thin gray
bar with tiny words. Reading left to right, you'll see:

     (Apple logo)   Safari   File   Edit   View   ...

Click the word **Safari** in that top bar. NOT the blue icon at the
bottom — the small word 'Safari' at the very top.

A dropdown menu will appear. Click **Settings...** in that dropdown."

A Settings window will pop up in the center of the screen.

**If they say "I don't see Settings":** They probably clicked the
Safari icon in the Dock instead of the word in the menu bar. Tell
them: "Look at the VERY TOP EDGE of your screen — there's a thin
bar up there with small text. That's what we need. Click the word
'Safari' in that thin top bar, not the icon at the bottom."

**If the menu bar is hidden:** Tell them: "Move your mouse cursor
to the very top edge of your screen and leave it there for a second.
The menu bar should slide down and appear."

---

### Step 3: Go to the Advanced tab

Tell the user:

"Great! A settings window just appeared. Look at the top of THAT
window — it has small icons or tabs across the top. They say things
like General, Tabs, Passwords, Search, Security, etc.

Click **Advanced**. It's the very last one, all the way to the right.
You might need to look carefully — it's at the far right end."

---

### Step 4: Check 'Show features for web developers'

Tell the user:

"You're now in the Advanced panel. Scroll down if needed. Look for
a checkbox that says:

     ☐ Show features for web developers

Click that checkbox so it gets a checkmark:  ☑

That's it for this window. Close it by clicking the red circle in
the top-left corner of the settings window."

This makes a new "Develop" menu appear in Safari's top menu bar.

---

### Step 5: Verify the Develop menu appeared

Tell the user:

"Now look at the top menu bar again — that thin bar at the very top
of your screen. Do you see a new word **Develop** up there? It should
appear between 'Bookmarks' and 'Window'."

**If they see it:** "Perfect! Click on 'Develop'."

**If they DON'T see it:** Walk them through this recovery:

"The checkbox might not have saved. Let's try again:

1. Close Safari completely — click the word 'Safari' in the top menu
   bar, then click 'Quit Safari' at the bottom of the dropdown.
   (Or press Command + Q on your keyboard.)
2. Wait 5 seconds.
3. Open Safari again (click the blue compass at the bottom).
4. Go back to Safari → Settings → Advanced (same steps as before).
5. Check whether 'Show features for web developers' still has a
   checkmark. If not, check it again.
6. Close settings and look for 'Develop' in the top bar."

If it STILL doesn't appear after reopening Safari, their macOS
version may put this in a different location. Tell them to search
every tab in Safari Settings for the word "developer."

---

### Step 6: Open Developer Settings

Tell the user:

"Click the word **Develop** in the top menu bar. A long dropdown
list will appear. Scroll all the way to the very bottom of that
list. The last item says **Developer Settings...** — click it."

A new settings panel will appear.

---

### Step 7: Allow JavaScript from Apple Events

**THIS IS THE MOST IMPORTANT STEP. NOTHING WORKS WITHOUT THIS.**

Before they click anything, explain what's about to happen:

"This next step is going to ask for your Mac password. I want to
explain why BEFORE it happens so you're not surprised:

Your Mac has a security feature that prevents programs from
controlling Safari without your permission. We're about to turn
on that permission. Your Mac will ask for your password to confirm
it's really you — the same password you type when you turn on your
computer. Nothing is sent anywhere. It stays on your Mac.

This is NOT a scam. This is NOT a virus. This is a built-in Apple
security prompt — the same kind of prompt you see when you install
an app or change system settings. Apple designed it this way to
protect you. You're just telling your Mac: 'Yes, I want to allow
this.'"

Now tell them:

"In the settings panel that just opened, look for a checkbox that
says:

     ☐ Allow JavaScript from Apple Events

Click that checkbox."

Then:

"A small window appeared asking for your password. Type the password
you use to log into your Mac (the one you type when you turn on your
computer or wake it from sleep). Then click **OK** or press Enter."

**If they clicked Cancel because they got scared:**
"No problem at all! The checkbox just unchecked itself. Let's try
again — click the checkbox one more time. When the password box
appears, type your Mac login password and click OK. I promise this
is safe — your Mac is just confirming it's you."

**If no password box appeared:**
The setting may have already been enabled from a previous setup.
That's fine — move on.

**If they say the password didn't work:**
"Make sure you're typing your Mac login password — the one you use
when you start your computer. Not an Apple ID password, not an
email password. The password for THIS computer."

---

### Step 8: Open a website in Safari

Tell the user:

"The hard part is done! Now just open a website in Safari that you
want me to help with. Type one of these into Safari's address bar
(the long bar at the top of the Safari window) and press Enter:

  - claude.ai — for AI chat
  - mail.google.com — for email
  - Or any other website you use

Make sure the website is fully loaded — you should see the actual
page, not a loading spinner. Log in if it asks you to."

The website must be open and visible in a Safari tab.

---

### Step 9: The automation permission popup

**WARN THE USER BEFORE YOU RUN ANYTHING.**

The popup will say "[APP NAME] wants to access and control Safari."
The app name depends on what terminal the user is running. It could
say ANY of these — they are ALL normal and safe:

| What the popup says | What it means |
|---------------------|---------------|
| "**Terminal** wants to control Safari" | They're using the built-in Mac terminal |
| "**Cursor** wants to control Safari" | They're using Cursor (AI code editor) |
| "**Windsurf** wants to control Safari" | They're using Windsurf (AI code editor) |
| "**Code** wants to control Safari" | They're using VS Code |
| "**iTerm2** wants to control Safari" | They're using iTerm |
| "**Warp** wants to control Safari" | They're using Warp terminal |

ALL of these are fine. The name just reflects whichever program is
running the script. It is NOT a virus. It is NOT a random program
trying to hack them. It's the tool they're already using.

Tell the user:

"I'm about to run a quick setup check. When I do, your Mac is going
to show a popup — a small white box near the top-center of your
screen. It will say something like:

     '[Some app name] wants to access and control Safari.'

The app name might say 'Terminal', or 'Cursor', or 'Windsurf', or
something else — that's just the name of the program we're using
right now to talk to each other. It's not a virus or a hack. It's
your Mac asking if it's OK for THIS program to read Safari pages.

There will be two buttons: 'Don't Allow' and 'Allow'.

**Click Allow.**

If you accidentally click 'Don't Allow', I'll tell you how to fix
it. Ready?"

Wait for them to say yes. Then run:

```bash
./preflight.sh
```

This takes about 5-10 seconds. Tell the user: "Running the check
now — give it a few seconds..."

**If the user says "it says [some name] wants to control Safari,
is that safe?":** Yes. Whatever name appears in that popup is the
terminal app they're currently using. Tell them: "That's the program
we're talking through right now. It's safe — click Allow."

---

### Step 9a: If the user clicked 'Don't Allow'

Tell them:

"No worries, easy fix. Here's what to do:

1. Click the **Apple logo** in the very top-left corner of your screen.
2. Click **System Settings** in the dropdown.
3. In the left sidebar, scroll down and click **Privacy & Security**.
4. Scroll down on the right side and click **Automation**.
5. You'll see a list of apps. Find the one you're using right now:
   - **Terminal** — the built-in Mac terminal (black icon with ">_")
   - **iTerm** or **iTerm2** — a popular alternative terminal
   - **Cursor** — AI code editor (purple icon)
   - **Windsurf** — AI code editor (blue wave icon)
   - **Code** — VS Code (blue icon)
   - **Warp** — another terminal app
   Not sure which one? It's whichever program you're reading this in.
6. Under that app name, you'll see **Safari** with a toggle switch.
   Turn that switch **ON** (it should turn blue or green).
7. Close System Settings."

Then run `./preflight.sh` again.

---

### Step 9b: Reading the preflight results

**If all checks pass:**
Tell the user: "Setup complete! I can now read and write to your
Safari tabs. Let me show you what I can do."

You'll see output like:
```
  [OK] Safari is running
  [OK] osascript can read Safari
  [OK] JavaScript from Apple Events
  [OK] tab.sh utility works
  [OK] Claude tab found (tab 1)
  [OK] JS execution in Claude tab 1

Result: 6 passed, 0 failed
All checks passed.
```

**If some checks fail, use this decision tree:**

❌ "Safari is not running"
→ Tell user: "Open Safari and make sure it's running."

❌ "osascript can read Safari" fails
→ This is the automation permission. Follow Step 9a above.

❌ "JavaScript from Apple Events" fails
→ Tell user: "We need to redo one step. Click 'Develop' in the top
  menu bar → 'Developer Settings' at the bottom → check the box for
  'Allow JavaScript from Apple Events' → enter your password."

❌ "tab.sh utility works" fails
→ Tell user: "Make sure Safari has at least one tab open with a real
  website loaded — not a blank tab."

❌ "Claude tab found" fails
→ Tell user: "Open a website in Safari — go to claude.ai, gmail.com,
  or any site you want me to work with."

❌ 3 or more checks failed
→ Tell user: "A few things didn't save. Let's start clean:
  1. Close Safari completely (Safari menu → Quit Safari, or Cmd+Q)
  2. Wait 5 seconds
  3. Open Safari again
  4. Go to Safari → Settings → Advanced → check 'Show features for
     web developers'
  5. Close settings
  6. Click Develop → Developer Settings → check 'Allow JavaScript
     from Apple Events' → enter your password
  7. Open a website (like claude.ai)
  Then I'll run the check again."

❌ ALL checks failed
→ Something fundamental is wrong. Ask: "What kind of Mac do you have?
  Click the Apple logo in the top-left → About This Mac. Tell me what
  it says." This helps diagnose macOS version issues.

---

### Step 10: Start being useful

Setup is done. Now IMMEDIATELY ask the user what they want to automate.
Say exactly this:

"Setup complete! wrapper-bot can now read and write to any website
you're logged into in Safari. Two things I can do right now:

1. **Manage your Gmail** — read your inbox, archive junk, star
   important mail, set up filters so it stays clean
2. **Watch a Claude conversation** — I can monitor a claude.ai tab
   and relay messages between your phone and this terminal

Which sounds useful? Or tell me any other website you want to automate."

IMPORTANT: Do NOT just show a list of scripts. Offer the USE CASES.
The user doesn't care about tab.sh — they care about inbox zero.

If the user picks Gmail (or mentions email, inbox, gmail, messages):
1. Run `./gmail-scan.sh` — this finds the Gmail tab and prints the inbox
2. If it says "Gmail is not open": tell user "Open mail.google.com in Safari"
3. Once you have the inbox list, show the user what's there
4. Offer to help: archive junk, star important, set up filters
5. To send email: `./gmail-compose.sh --to "addr" --subject "subj" --body "msg"`

If the user picks Claude watching:
```bash
nohup ./watch.sh > /tmp/wrapper-watch.log 2>&1 &
```
Then explain: "Open claude.ai on your phone. Type what you want me
to do. I'll see it within 15 seconds."

Script reference (for your use, not the user's):

Read any tab:
```bash
./scrape.sh --raw                    # front tab
./scrape.sh --raw --tab 1            # specific tab by index
```

Write to any tab:
```bash
./post.sh "Hello from your AI assistant"
./post.sh --tab 1 "message"
```

Send email (needs Gmail open in a tab):
```bash
./gmail-compose.sh --to "someone@example.com" --subject "Hello" --body "Message"
```

---

## The user flow in plain english

```
User opens Safari and logs into a website
    ↓
You run ./scrape.sh — now you can read everything on that page
    ↓
You run ./post.sh "message" — now you can type into that page
    ↓
You run ./watch.sh — now you auto-detect when the page changes
    ↓
You are a full browser automation agent using eight shell scripts
```

## Script reference

| Script | What it does | Example |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| `tab.sh` | Find which tab has which website | `./tab.sh find "gmail.com"` → `2` |
| `scrape.sh` | Read all text from a Safari tab | `./scrape.sh --raw --tab 1` |
| `post.sh` | Type a message and hit send | `./post.sh --tab 1 "Hi"` |
| `gmail-compose.sh` | Compose and send an email | `./gmail-compose.sh --to "x@y.com" --subject "Hi" --body "Hello"` |
| `watch.sh` | Watch for changes, save to files | `./watch.sh` (runs forever) |
| `preflight.sh` | Check if setup is complete | `./preflight.sh --gmail` |

## Requirements

- macOS (any version with Safari)
- Safari with "Allow JavaScript from Apple Events" enabled
- A website open in a Safari tab (logged in)
- That's it. No npm. No pip. No Docker. No API keys.

---

## Use Case 1: Phone-to-Terminal (Claude Dictation)

The user wants to control their Mac terminal from their phone via claude.ai.

How to set it up:
1. User opens claude.ai in a Safari tab on their Mac
2. Run `./watch.sh` in the background — it polls that tab every 15 seconds
3. User opens claude.ai on their PHONE and types instructions
4. watch.sh detects the new message, saves it to `chatsource/`
5. The AI assistant on the Mac (Claude Code, Cursor, etc.) reads the
   message and acts on it — runs tests, checks builds, deploys, etc.

This is a read loop. The Mac watches for phone-typed messages.
The AI on the Mac does whatever the message says.

Example:
```bash
# Start watching (background)
nohup ./watch.sh > /tmp/wrapper-watch.log 2>&1 &

# The AI checks for new messages by reading the latest chatsource file
ls -t chatsource/*.txt | head -1 | xargs cat
```

Tell the user: "Open claude.ai on your phone. Type what you want your
Mac to do. I'll see it within 15 seconds and run it for you."

---

## Use Case 2: Gmail Inbox Helper

The user wants their AI to manage their Gmail inbox.

Prerequisites: Gmail open in a Safari tab (logged in).

### Reading inbox (ONE COMMAND)

```bash
./gmail-scan.sh
```

This prints up to 30 emails, one per line:
```
[UNREAD] Jeffrey Robbins | AI Is Hitting the Grid Wall | 1:01 PM
[read]   Amazon.com | Shipped: "Freshmage Fruit Storage..." | 3:30 AM
```

Quick count: `./gmail-scan.sh --count` → "1 unread out of 100 visible"

If it says "Gmail is not open" → tell the user to open mail.google.com in Safari.

### Sending email

```bash
./gmail-compose.sh \
    --to "recipient@example.com" \
    --subject "Subject line" \
    --body "Message body"
```

### Inbox management via tab.sh js

Use `./tab.sh js` to run JavaScript directly in the Gmail tab:

```bash
GMAIL_TAB=$(./tab.sh find "mail.google.com")

# Count unread emails
./tab.sh js "$GMAIL_TAB" "document.querySelectorAll('tr.zE').length"

# Click "Select all" checkbox
./tab.sh js "$GMAIL_TAB" "(function(){var c=document.querySelector('div[gh=\"tm\"] span[role=\"checkbox\"]');if(c)c.click();return c?'SELECTED':'NOT_FOUND'})()"

# Click Archive button
./tab.sh js "$GMAIL_TAB" "(function(){var a=document.querySelector('div[act=\"7\"]');if(a)a.click();return a?'ARCHIVED':'NOT_FOUND'})()"
```

### Creating filters

Navigate to Gmail filter settings, then use tab.sh js to fill filter
criteria and check action boxes:

```bash
# Navigate to filters
./tab.sh js "$GMAIL_TAB" "window.location.hash='#settings/filters'"

# After navigating: create, fill criteria, check action boxes via JS
# For selecting a label from the dropdown: walk the user through
# that one click. Everything else is automated.
```

When creating filters, the AI can automate these actions:
- Skip the Inbox (Archive it)
- Star it
- Mark as read
- Never send to Spam
- Always mark as important
- Never mark as important

For applying a label: tell the user "Click the label name in the
dropdown — I've already filled everything else." One click per filter.

### Recommended filter setup for inbox zero

Walk the user through creating these filters:

| From / Has words | Action |
|-----------------|--------|
| `notifications@github.com` | Archive, never spam |
| `noreply@` | Archive, never spam |
| `*@substack.com` | Archive, never spam |
| `unsubscribe` (has words) | Archive, never spam |
| Domains the user cares about | Star, mark important |

Tell the user: "I'll set up filters so junk gets archived automatically
and important mail gets starred. Your inbox will only show things that
need your attention."

---
